C:
For bursty traffic, start with serverless functions (AWS Lambda, Cloud Run functions, Azure Functions). The traffic pattern is exactly what serverless is built for, and you'd have to hit specific pain points before containers become the better call.

Here's the reasoning.

**Why serverless fits bursty traffic specifically**

Bursty means long stretches of low or zero traffic punctuated by sharp spikes. That pattern punishes containers on both ends: you either over-provision (paying for idle capacity during the quiet periods) or you under-provision and eat latency while autoscalers and new nodes spin up to meet a spike. Serverless scales per-request from zero to thousands of concurrent executions in seconds, and you pay only for actual invocations. During the quiet periods your cost is essentially zero; during a spike you get capacity without having pre-paid for it.

**The trade-offs you're accepting**

- *Cold starts.* The first invocation after idle adds latency (tens of ms for lightweight runtimes, up to a few seconds for heavy JVM/.NET or large dependency bundles). If your API has tight p99 latency requirements on the *first* request after a lull, this is the main thing to test. Provisioned concurrency mitigates it but erodes the cost advantage.
- *Execution limits.* Functions cap request duration (Lambda is 15 min) and have memory ceilings. Fine for typical request/response APIs; a problem for long-running or streaming work.
- *Cost crossover at sustained high volume.* Per-request pricing is cheap when usage is spiky but gets *more* expensive than containers once you're running near-constant high load. Bursty traffic by definition isn't that — but if the baseline creeps up over time, re-evaluate.

**When I'd choose containers instead**

Flip to containers (or a hybrid) if any of these are true: you need sub-100ms p99 with no cold-start tolerance, requests routinely run longer than the function timeout, you have heavy/specialized runtime or GPU needs, you want to avoid vendor lock-in, or your "bursty" load actually has a high constant floor. A common middle ground is **Cloud Run / AWS App Runner / container-based serverless**, which scales containers to zero like functions but gives you more control over runtime, memory, and request duration — that's often the best of both for a bursty API that's slightly too heavy for plain functions.

**My concrete recommendation:** begin with serverless functions, and benchmark cold-start latency against your p99 target early — that single test is what tells you whether to stay on plain functions, add provisioned concurrency, or step up to a scale-to-zero container platform.

If you tell me the cloud provider, expected peak concurrency, and your latency target, I can make this specific rather than general.

For bursty traffic, start with serverless functions (AWS Lambda, Cloud Run functions, Azure Functions). The traffic pattern is exactly what serverless is built for, and you'd have to hit specific pain points before containers become the better call.

Here's the reasoning.

**Why serverless fits bursty traffic specifically**

Bursty means long stretches of low or zero traffic punctuated by sharp spikes. That pattern punishes containers on both ends: you either over-provision (paying for idle capacity during the quiet periods) or you under-provision and eat latency while autoscalers and new nodes spin up to meet a spike. Serverless scales per-request from zero to thousands of concurrent executions in seconds, and you pay only for actual invocations. During the quiet periods your cost is essentially zero; during a spike you get capacity without having pre-paid for it.

**The trade-offs you're accepting**

- *Cold starts.* The first invocation after idle adds latency (tens of ms for lightweight runtimes, up to a few seconds for heavy JVM/.NET or large dependency bundles). If your API has tight p99 latency requirements on the *first* request after a lull, this is the main thing to test. Provisioned concurrency mitigates it but erodes the cost advantage.
- *Execution limits.* Functions cap request duration (Lambda is 15 min) and have memory ceilings. Fine for typical request/response APIs; a problem for long-running or streaming work.
- *Cost crossover at sustained high volume.* Per-request pricing is cheap when usage is spiky but gets *more* expensive than containers once you're running near-constant high load. Bursty traffic by definition isn't that — but if the baseline creeps up over time, re-evaluate.

**When I'd choose containers instead**

Flip to containers (or a hybrid) if any of these are true: you need sub-100ms p99 with no cold-start tolerance, requests routinely run longer than the function timeout, you have heavy/specialized runtime or GPU needs, you want to avoid vendor lock-in, or your "bursty" load actually has a high constant floor. A common middle ground is **Cloud Run / AWS App Runner / container-based serverless**, which scales containers to zero like functions but gives you more control over runtime, memory, and request duration — that's often the best of both for a bursty API that's slightly too heavy for plain functions.

**What would change this recommendation**

This answer holds only as long as the traffic is genuinely bursty and the workload is a standard request/response API. The conclusion flips if any of the following turns out to be true: the cold-start benchmark misses your p99 target even with provisioned concurrency (containers win on latency); the "bursty" load actually has a high constant floor or trends toward sustained high volume (containers win on cost); or requests run long, stream, or need GPU/specialized runtimes (functions can't do the job). The single most likely thing to overturn it is the cold-start measurement — if first-request latency after a lull breaks your SLA, stop reading serverless as the default. If your traffic isn't truly bursty in the first place, the whole premise is wrong and you should size for the real baseline instead.

**My concrete recommendation:** begin with serverless functions, and benchmark cold-start latency against your p99 target early — that single test is what tells you whether to stay on plain functions, add provisioned concurrency, or step up to a scale-to-zero container platform.

P:
Use a **serverless platform that scales to zero** — and for an API specifically, default to a managed container service (Google Cloud Run, AWS App Runner, or Azure Container Apps) rather than plain functions. Reserve always-on containers (ECS/EKS/GKE with a running node pool) for the specific cases below. The reasoning, and the one test that settles it, follow.

**First, dissolve the binary.** "Serverless functions vs. containers" is no longer the real fork. The fork that matters for bursty traffic is **scale-to-zero vs. always-provisioned**. Both functions (Lambda, Azure Functions, Cloud Run functions) and managed container platforms (Cloud Run, App Runner, Container Apps) scale to zero and bill per use; a self-managed container cluster does not. So the question becomes two smaller ones: should you scale to zero (yes, for bursty), and if so, functions or containers (containers, for most APIs).

**Why scale-to-zero fits bursty traffic.** Bursty means long stretches of low or zero load punctuated by sharp spikes. That pattern punishes always-on capacity on both ends: over-provision and you pay for idle machines through every quiet stretch; under-provision and you eat latency while autoscalers add nodes to catch a spike. Scale-to-zero platforms add capacity per request — from zero to thousands of concurrent executions in seconds — and bill only for what runs. Quiet periods cost ~nothing; spikes get served without pre-paying for headroom.

**Why containers over functions, for an API.** A managed container platform gives you the same burst economics as functions while removing the function-specific friction: no 15-minute request ceiling, your normal web framework and runtime unchanged, ordinary Docker-based local dev, larger memory/CPU options, and no cloud rewrite if the service later grows. Drop to plain **functions** instead when the workload is genuinely event-driven or glue code (queue handlers, webhooks, cron, light request/response) and you want the absolute minimum operational surface. Both are "serverless"; the container variant is simply the better-fitting shape for a real API.

**The trade-offs you're accepting** (true of either scale-to-zero option):
- *Cold starts.* The first request after idle adds latency — typically tens to low-hundreds of milliseconds for Node/Python/Go, up to several seconds for JVM/.NET or large dependency bundles. If you have a tight p99 target on that first post-idle request, this is the thing to measure. Minimum-instance / provisioned-concurrency settings remove it but reintroduce a small always-on cost, eroding the scale-to-zero advantage.
- *Cost crossover at sustained load.* Per-use pricing is cheap when traffic is spiky but becomes *more* expensive than reserved containers once you run near-constant high utilization. Bursty traffic by definition isn't that — but if a steady baseline creeps up over months, re-check the math.
- *Per-request execution model.* Concurrency, timeouts, and statelessness are platform-governed. Fine for standard request/response APIs; constraining for long-lived connections, heavy streaming, or in-process background work.

**When always-on containers are the right call instead.** Choose a running cluster (or a hybrid: reserved baseline + serverless burst) if any of these hold: you need sub-100ms p99 with zero cold-start tolerance and can't accept the cost of keeping warm instances; requests routinely outlast the platform timeout; you have specialized or GPU runtime needs; or your "bursty" load actually sits on a high constant floor, in which case the spiky-pricing advantage never materializes.

**The decisive test.** Benchmark cold-start latency against your p99 target before committing. That single measurement tells you whether to run plain scale-to-zero, add a warm minimum-instance floor, or move to always-on containers. Everything above is the default; this is what confirms it for your workload.

Tell me the cloud provider, expected peak concurrency, and your p99 latency target, and I'll turn this into a specific platform-and-config recommendation with rough cost figures.
